title: Sub-picosecond inter-core skew characterization in multicore fibers via Hong--Ou--Mandel interference
authors: L. Lira Tacca, L. Marques Fagundes, M. Morales Lillo, M. Navarro, I. Machuca, S. Gómez, G. H. dos Santos, J. Cariñe, G. Saavedra, E. S. Gómez, G. Lima, S. P. Walborn
abstract: Inter-core skew (ICS), the differential group delay between cores of a multicore fiber (MCF), is a critical parameter for both classical space-division multiplexed communications and quantum photonic networks. We present a high-precision measurement of ICS in a commercially available four-core fiber using two-photon Hong--Ou--Mandel (HOM) interference in a fiber-integrated $4\times4$ multiport beam splitter. By extracting the center position of HOM interference dips and peaks across all twelve core-pair combinations, we obtain individual ICS values with a demonstrated precision of $\pm0.11\,$ps, limited by the delay-stage positioning uncertainty. The root-mean-square ICS grows as $σ_τ(L) = κ\sqrt{L}+c$ with $κ= 48.7 \pm 2.5\,\mathrm{ps}/\!\sqrt{\mathrm{km}}$ and $c = 9.76 \pm 1.2\,$ps, over fiber lengths from $7.7\,$m to $1300\,$m. This first direct validation of the stochastic random-walk scaling across a length range spanning laboratory to field-deployed scales was made possible by HOM's immunity to first-order path fluctuations, which renders classical interferometric methods impractical for long installed fibers. The demonstrated $\pm0.11\,$ps precision represents a $\sim\!180$-fold improvement over correlation optical time-domain reflectometry (C-OTDR), the standard method for long-fiber ICS characterization. Fisher information analysis establishes a fundamental Cramér--Rao precision limit in the femtosecond range, indicating further improvement is achievable with better delay control. These results establish a practical platform for characterising timing uniformity in MCF-based networks for both quantum and classical space-division multiplexed applications.
summary: This paper presents a novel, high-precision method to measure inter-core skew (ICS) in multicore fibers using two-photon Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference. By achieving sub-picosecond resolution, the authors validated the expected random-walk scaling of ICS over long distances. This technique establishes a practical platform for ensuring timing uniformity in MCFs for both classical and quantum space-division multiplexing applications.
main_problem: The paper addresses the critical need to accurately characterize Inter-core Skew (ICS), which is the differential group delay between cores in Multicore Fibers (MCFs), for use in quantum photonic networks.
main_result: The authors achieved a high-precision measurement of ICS with a demonstrated accuracy of $\pm 0.11$ ps, validating the stochastic random-walk scaling of this delay over long fiber lengths.
method: The measurement relies on two-photon Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference using either Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC) or pulsed lasers to probe the differential group delay.
model_or_system: The physical system is a multicore fiber (MCF), and the measurement utilizes HOM interference in a multiport beam splitter. The ICS is modeled as a zero-mean random walk, leading to an RMS growth $\sigma_{	au}(L) \propto \sqrt{L}$.
key_observables: Inter-core Skew ($	au_{jk}$), HOM interference dip/peak center positions, and the Root Mean Square (RMS) ICS scaling $\sigma_{	au}(L)$.
important_parameters: Measurement precision of $\pm 0.11$ ps; scaling coefficient $\kappa = 48.7 \pm 2.5 	ext{ ps}/\sqrt{	ext{km}}$; length range from $7.7 	ext{ m}$ to $1300 	ext{ m}$.
main_assumptions: The HOM dip/peak shift is directly proportional to the differential group delay, and the measurement is limited by the delay-stage positioning uncertainty.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the HOM interference data, showing the characteristic bunching dips and anti-bunching peaks for different fiber lengths and sources (SPDC vs. pulsed laser).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ICS characterization, details the HOM interferometric method using both SPDC and pulsed lasers, presents the extraction of ICS from interference features, and finally validates the stochastic random-walk scaling over a wide length range.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial, high-precision diagnostic tool for characterizing timing uniformity in optical fibers, which is essential for scaling quantum communication networks that rely on precise temporal synchronization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Illustration of ICS accumulation in a MCF. Identical pulses propagate (here from left to right) through different cores of the MCF. Because both pulses have the same frequency spectrum, any accumulated differential delay arises solely from core-dependent group velocity variations.

Fig. 2. a) Experimental setup for HOM interference using SPDC source. The photons are coupled into SMFs, separated by a variable delay 𝜏, and injected into the four-core MCF via a FAN-IN. Coincidence counts are registered by SNSPDs and an FPGA. b) Experimental setup for HOM interference using a weak laser source. Additional optical delay lines were added. See text for additional details.

Fig. 3. HOM interference data. (a) Normalised coincidence counts for all six output pairs as a function of relative delay for a 12.7 m MCF, input combination 𝑎3,𝑎4, using the SPDC source. Solid lines are Gaussian fits. Statistical error bars are smaller than the data points and not visible at this scale. Coincidence curves show bunching dips (AC, AD, BC, BD) and anti-bunching peaks (AB, CD) as predicted by the 4 × 4 Hadamard transformation. Fit parameters and visibilities are listed in Table 1.
